dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a "Diabetic Problem" at a residence.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led to the 911 call today?
I have dizziness, generalized weakness, and my blood sugar went up to nearly 600 mg/dL earlier.
When did your blood sugar spike, and what did you do for it?
Along with dizziness, when did the chest discomfort start, and is it on one side?
You mentioned taking nitroglycerin for the chest discomfort. Did it help a bit afterward?
The discomfort is still present, but not worsening. That right?
Did you take any other medications after the nitroglycerin was used?
Can you open your eyes on your own and tell me the year and location?
You are aware of your name, today's date, and current city. Is that accurate?
When I asked you to squeeze my fingers one at a time, you were able to do both sides.
Right now, your airway is clear and you are breathing without signs of acute distress.
You have clear bilateral lung sounds with no rales, rhonchi, or wheezing.
Your skin is dry and warm with strong peripheral pulses in both hands and legs.
Your current blood pressure is 172 over 90-slightly elevated.
Heart rate is 92 beats per minute and regular in rhythm.
Respirations are 21 per minute, regular, and even.
Oxygen saturation is at 100% while on room air, no need for extra support.
A fingerstick test shows a blood sugar level of 479 mg/dL, which is very high.
Placing you on 4-lead EKG now. We plan to get a 12-lead EKG shortly for further evaluation.
No new focal neurological findings-no nuchal rigidity or head swelling.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the current chest discomfort?
Right now you're on drugs like Humalog, Levemir, Metoprolol, Topamax, Lipitor, Albuterol, and Nitroglycerin.
You do not have any known drug or food allergies noted in your medical record.
You have a history of stroke, hypertension, type 1 diabetes, asthma, and epilepsy.
An IV was placed in your left forearm during the first attempt-verified patent with blood aspiration.
One 12-lead EKG was attempted but showed movement artifact, however, no AMI or ischemic signs were noted.
_reporting chest pain is 8 out of 10, dull and localized to the left anterior chest.
Repeating vitals showing BP of 145/89 with respiration at 27 per minute.
SpO2 is at 98%-still on room air, not a significant change.
Pulse now is 82 and regular, but the glucometer is not providing a reading right now.
We are helping you carefully onto the stretcher due to your weakness and the reported issues in your legs.
IV and EKG monitoring remain stable and ready for transport.
On a 0 to 10 pain scale-still at 8, not any improvement or worsening, just dull and constant.
Tenderness is reported only on the left anterior side, not reproducible and non-radiating.
Let me confirm-your symptoms began after taking 8 units of Humalog for elevated sugar, correct?
No fever, cough, falls, or injuries were mentioned-confirmed?
We gave normal saline IV per protocol for high blood sugar; your condition seems to have improved.
IV is patent and flowing without issues; no signs of infiltration or problems.
We're taking you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation and monitoring.
IV line and monitoring are stable and secured for safe transport.
IV fluids are running at a maintenance rate now to help support hydration.
Will continue to monitor your vitals and repeat a 12-lead EKG if needed in transit.
The 12-lead EKG showed small R waves in V3 and minimal anterior vector-nothing indicating AMI or ischemic event.
Also noted occasional ventricular ectopy with acceptable artifact reduction in the rhythms.
Transport initiated under Protocol 3-7 for hyperglycemia with chest discomfort and negative 12-lead findings.
GCS remains 15-eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6. Patient alert and oriented.
IV secured and fluid rate set to prevent sudden shifts due to patient's hypoglycemic concerns.
Hospital staff are aware of patient's status and next EKG plan before arrival.
All gear is double-checked and ready for ambulance transfer.
IV placed in an extremity vein and away from central areas as per safety standards.
The patient cannot take aspirin due to reported dizziness and headache.
We noted that your speech is slurred and delayed, as you describe it's normal for your elevated BSL.
No issues evident in the GU area and reported no recent incontinence.
You can move both arms as tested, but noted weakness during mobility; no deformity or angulation.
Patient's skin appears dry, warm, and with no rash, bite marks, or bruising noted.
Patient is also on Butalbital, Codeine, Caffeine, and Atorvastatin as per patient history.
EKG shows nonspecific T wave abnormalities and possible left atrial enlargement per tracing.
Patient refuses to take additional NTG due to headache and dizziness already experienced.
No signs of pericarditis, murmurs, or abnormal heart sounds are heard.
Repeating vitals-BP is now 145/89 with regular respiratory features.
GCS remains stable-15 with orientation intact.
Patient has a documented history of seizures, though no seizure noted at this time.
Short QT interval is also noted in current test-non-specific finding reported.
ED staff will be prepared for a patient with diabetes and a history of CVA and minor cardiac issues.
Ready for ambulance transfer after confirming all equipment and patient stability.
Patient has not changed status; no deterioration in EKG or vital signs since last repeat check.
No serious signs of hemodynamic instability or neurologic changes observed.
Last meal was approximately two days ago with no intake after elevated blood sugar.
Some T wave inversion is noted along with sinus rhythm, but no acute ST changes.
IV works good and no compromise detected during motion or transport setup.
Patient left in the living room with family present before transfer to the stretcher in proper sequence.
No contraindications noted to normal saline given, and IV remains patent for transport.
EKG monitor functions are tested and confirmed functional and stable for ambulance transport.
Patient is alert and responding, GCS high, no signs of CVA or stroke during evaluation.
No visible abnormal scars, strains, or signs of effort in back or lower extremities.
Skin remains dry, warm, and with good turgor, rechecking entire body during motion.
No phlebitis or infiltration at IV site even after repeated checks.
Blood pressure, respiratory rate, and pulse rate fluctuate within normal variation.
No signs of chest pain worsening during movement to stretcher or previous checks.
Nitroglycerin given by patient did yield minor relief but not sufficient to make them stable.
No acute hypoglycemia evident with glucose at unreadable, but patient seems stable since IV fluids.
No signs of AMI, despite small R in V3, and no other acute changes noted on serial EKGs.
No new focal neurological deficits seen in re-check; slurred speech reported per the patient regularly.
Patient denied fever, cough, chills, or any recent injury or trauma by any means.
No change in lung sounds with findings remained equal and clear bilaterally both times.
No change in motor skills or sensory function during movement test and retests.
No focal weakness reported after movement, but generalized weakness ongoing as seen before.
Patient's insurance status verified and loaded electronically before leaving premises.
Transfer to ED bed done with patient self-moving with minimal help, no acute need of assistance.
No signs of breathing difficulties or discomfort during and after IV and EKG procedures.
No improvement noted but no worsening in chest discomfort per patient's direct reports.
No spontaneous movements or EMG signs of seizure or CVA in current observation.
No further signs of clot or possible CV readiness; protocol chosen to be appropriate.
Patient declined additional medications due to headache and described dizziness complaints.
All findings and history go in line with current protocol and do not suggest otherwise.
Readiness for transfers done; ambulance and patient all prepped for departure to receiving facility.
All equipment placed and secured-readiness confirmed for moving to
